---
title: "DEEP: Future Fit"
author: "by Jess"
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: [solarized, custom.scss]
        slideNumber: true
        show-slide-number: all
        #reference-location: document
        #logo: images/logo.png
        footer: "https://deep-future-fit.netlify.app/"
        chalkboard:
            theme: whiteboard
        #code-annotations: hover
        css: styles.css
        mermaid:
          theme: default
        highlight-style: github
        #highlight-style: monochrome
        #logo: qr-code.png
        #multiplex: true
editor:
    render-on-save: true

---

# Review

# Gotta Go Fast

::: {.notes}
lossless but slow
:::

## Protocol Race

1. Get into groups of three and decide who will be the *client*, the *server*, and the *link*.
    - The link will be running!!!

2. The client and server should stand on opposite sides of the field.

3. Server will have a buffer of X bytes. Each turn, it can only remove N bytes from its buffer. Only the server will know X and N.

## Round Start
1. Client writes the number of bytes it wants to send to the server, and the link will deliver the message.

2. The server when receiving a message should:
    - Remove up to N bytes in the buffer
    - Check if there is space for incoming bytes, and if there is, add them to the buffer and write a check mark on the whiteboard
    - If there is no space, do not add any and write an X on the whiteboard

##
If the client receives an X, it can't send anything for 10 seconds.

<br>

If the client receives a checkmark, it can move up however many steps it sent as bytes to the server.

<br>

The first client to reach the server wins.

# Discussion

# TCP

::: {.notes}
switch to ppt slides
:::

# Cybersecurity

## Mizi and Sua

Mizi wants to send a message to Sua, but...

![](./images/mizi-sua.png){fig-align="center" height=200}


::: {.fragment .fade-in}
it has to go through me.

<br>

What should she do?
:::

::: {.notes}
traditionally known as alice and bob

get a volunteer in the class and try to do this
:::

## Hashing

A hash function takes in a message (preimage) and returns a fixed-size message (hash value/digest)

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
[strong tomato -> f4bc6e95368298d51a08...]{.highlight-red}
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
- same input = same output
- different input = different output?
- hard to reverse

Good default hash function as of June 2, 2026: [SHA256]{.highlight-red}

Avoid MD5!
:::

## Ciphers
An algorithm that obfuscates information so that it seems random to anyone who does not possess [the key]{.highlight-red}.

- e.g. Caesar cipher

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Symmetric encryption are ciphers that use the **same random** key to encrypt and decrypt.

<br>

Good default cipher as of June 2, 2026: [AES-256]{.highlight-red}
:::

::: {.notes}
explain caesar cipher

"the key", explain kerchkoff's principle
:::

## Asymmetric Encryption

- Every user has a public and private key
- The private and public keys reveal nothing about the other
- Messages encrypted with one key can only be decrypted with the other key

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Encryption: the sender encrypts the message with the recipient's public key.

Decryption: the recipient decrypts the message with their private key.

Good default cipher as of June 2, 2026: [RSA]{.highlight-red}
:::


## Signatures

Proof that a message was sent by a particular person

- The sender signs the message with their private key
- The recipient verifies the signature with the sender's public key

## SSL

An SSL certificate contains a website's public key among other details.

- The format itself is called X.509

It is issued by a Certificate Authority (CA) whose certificate is installed in your computer.

It allows your browser to verify the identity of a website.

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
[You have to trust someone or something at some point.]{.highlight-red}
:::

::: {.notes}
can demo the certificate thing in browser

can also demo installed certificates
:::

# Q & A

# Graduation

# Showcase
